title: End of module: Linear Algebra for Signals

questions.text: A nonzero vector v satisfies Mv = λv. What are v and λ called?
choices: v is an eigenvector of M, and λ is its eigenvalue
v is the determinant of M, and λ is its trace
v is a quadratic form, and λ is a covariance matrix
questions.explanation: By definition, a nonzero vector v satisfying Mv = λv for some scalar λ is an eigenvector with eigenvalue λ — multiplication by M scales v without rotating it.

questions.text: What does the spectral theorem guarantee for a real symmetric matrix?
choices: Its eigenvalues are always real, and it has an orthogonal set of eigenvectors
questions.explanation: The spectral theorem guarantees real eigenvalues and an orthogonal eigenbasis for any real symmetric matrix — exactly why covariance matrices (always symmetric) have well-behaved, real, comparable variances along their principal directions.

questions.text: For M = [[6,0],[0,2]] (diagonal), what is the larger eigenvalue?
questions.explanation: For a diagonal matrix, the eigenvalues are simply the diagonal entries themselves: 6 and 2, so the larger is 6.

questions.text: For M = [[3,2],[2,3]], the characteristic equation is λ² − 6λ + 5 = 0. What is the smaller eigenvalue?
questions.explanation: λ² − 6λ + 5 = 0 factors as (λ−1)(λ−5) = 0, giving eigenvalues 1 and 5. The smaller is 1.

questions.text: For a real covariance matrix Σ and any real vector a, why is a^T Σ a always ≥ 0?
choices: Because a^T Σ a equals Var(a^T x), the variance of a projection of the data — and variance can never be negative
Because covariance matrices always have a positive determinant
Because a must always be a unit vector
questions.explanation: a^T Σ a is exactly the variance of the scalar random variable a^T x, and variance is never negative — this is what forces every real covariance matrix to be positive semi-definite, with no exceptions.

questions.text: A symmetric matrix is positive semi-definite exactly when:
questions.explanation: Writing any vector in the matrix's eigenbasis turns the quadratic form into a weighted sum of squares, weighted by the eigenvalues — that sum stays non-negative for every possible vector only if every eigenvalue itself is non-negative.

questions.text: M = [[2,3],[3,2]] has eigenvalues found from λ² − 4λ − 5 = 0. Is the smaller eigenvalue ≥ 0? Enter the smaller eigenvalue (it may be negative).
questions.explanation: λ² − 4λ − 5 = 0 factors as (λ−5)(λ+1) = 0, giving eigenvalues 5 and −1. Since −1 is negative, this matrix is NOT positive semi-definite — it could never be a genuine real covariance matrix.

questions.text: For a covariance matrix Σ = [[a,b],[b,c]] with a = c and b ≠ 0, what is the principal eigenvector's tilt angle?
choices: Exactly 45°, regardless of the specific nonzero value of b
It depends only on b, never on a or c
questions.explanation: When a = c, the formula θ = ½·arctan(2b/(a−c)) has a zero denominator, corresponding to arctan's vertical asymptote (90°), giving θ = 45° for any nonzero b — only the eigenvalues' spread (not the tilt angle) depends on b's magnitude in this equal-variance case.

questions.text: One phrase (two words): the geometric/statistical technique that IS covariance-matrix eigendecomposition under a different name, used to find a dataset's dominant direction of variance, is called ___ ___ analysis.
questions.explanation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is exactly covariance-matrix eigendecomposition: the first principal component is the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue, the direction of greatest variance.
